[[Charlie Stone (rugby league)|Charlie Stone]] (uncle) }} }} '''Paul Newlove''' (born 10 August 1971) is an English former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1980s, 1990s and 2000s. He played as a {{rlp|ce}}, for [[Featherstone Rovers]], [[Bradford Northern]], [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] and [[Castleford Tigers]]. He also won 20 international caps for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]], and 7 caps for [[England national rugby league team|England]], including at the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup]]. ==Background== Newlove was born in [[Pontefract]], [[West Riding of Yorkshire]], England. ==Playing career== ===Featherstone Rovers=== Newlove made his début for Featherstone Rovers on Tuesday 27 September 1988, he later played at {{rlp|ce}} in [[Featherstone Rovers]]' 14-20 defeat by [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford Northern]] in the [[1989 Yorkshire Cup]] Final during the [[1989–90 Rugby Football League season|1989–90 season]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], [[Leeds]] on Sunday 5 November 1989. He moved on to play for [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford Northern]] five years later. Newlove played at {{rlp|ce}} and scored two [[Try (rugby)|tries]] in [[Featherstone Rovers]]' 20–16 victory over [[Workington Town]] in the [[1992–93 Rugby League Divisional Premiership|1992–93 Divisional Premiership]] Final at [[Old Trafford]], Manchester on 19 May 1993.{{cite web|url=http://www.btinternet.com/~Rovers/gpgg11.htm|title=Rovers Dig Deep To Lift The 1993 RL Premiership|publisher=Great Players – Great Games|date=31 December 2011|access-date=1 January 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121011225606/http://www.btinternet.com/~Rovers/gpgg11.htm|archive-date=11 October 2012}} Newlove scored a total of 48 tries during the season – a club record.{{cite web|title=Records|url=http://www.featherstonerovers.net/page.php?id=1097|publisher=Featherstone Rovers Rugby League Football Club|access-date=20 May 2014}} ===Bradford Northern=== [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford Northern]] paid £245,000{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/5107598.stm|title=Offiah hails arrival of Fielden|publisher=bbc.co.uk|date=22 June 2006|access-date=1 January 2012}} for Paul Newlove when he moved from Featherstone Rovers in 1993 (based on increases in average earnings, this would be approximately £486,900 in 2013). The transfer of Paul Newlove to St. Helens from the Bradford Bulls is still one of the most expensive rugby league transfers. ===St Helens=== [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] on 29 November 1995 paid Bradford Northern £250,000 cash (based on increases in average earnings, this would be approximately £464,900 in 2013),{{cite web|url=http://www.measuringworth.com/ukcompare|title=Measuring Worth – Relative Value of UK Pounds|publisher=Measuring Worth|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015}} and also sent [[Sonny Nickle]], [[Bernard Dwyer]] and [[Paul Loughlin]] to Bradford Northern. Newlove played for St Helens at centre in their [[1996 Challenge Cup]] Final victory over [[Bradford Bulls]].{{cite web|title=steveprescottfoundation.co.uk|url=http://www.steveprescottfoundation.co.uk/About/StevePrescottStats.aspx|work=Steve Prescott Stats|publisher=Steve Prescott Foundation|access-date=3 June 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120808052156/http://www.steveprescottfoundation.co.uk/About/StevePrescottStats.aspx|archive-date=8 August 2012|url-status=dead}} At the end of Super League's first season, Newlove was the season's top try scorer and was named at centre in the 1996 [[Super League Dream Team]]. Newlove played for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] at centre in their [[1999 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Bradford Bulls]]. Newlove played at {{rlp|ce}} and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]' 16-25 defeat by [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1995–96 League Cup (rugby league)|1995–96 Regal Trophy]] Final during the [[1995–96 Rugby Football League season|1995–96]] at [[John Smith's Stadium|Alfred McAlpine Stadium]], [[Huddersfield]] on Saturday 13 January 1996.{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|title=13th January 1996: St Helens 16 Wigan 25 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181007005311/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2976|archive-date=7 October 2018|url-status=dead}} Having won the 1999 Championship [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] contested in the [[2000 World Club Challenge]] against [[National Rugby League]] Premiers the [[Melbourne Storm]], with Newlove playing at centre in the loss. As [[Super League V]] champions, [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] played against [[2000 NRL season|2000 NRL]] Premiers, the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in the [[2001 World Club Challenge]]. Newlove played at centre in Saints' victory. Newlove had spent nine seasons at [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], lifting many trophies throughout a successful [[Knowsley Road]] career. Newlove played for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] at centre in their [[2002 Super League Grand Final]] victory against the [[Bradford Bulls]]. He joined [[Castleford Tigers]] in 2003, but made only five appearances for the club before retiring in May 2004 due to a recurring foot injury.{{cite web |title=Injured Newlove retires |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/castleford/3745441.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=31 July 2025 |date=25 May 2004}} ===Representative career=== Newlove made his international debut on 21 October 1989, becoming [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]]'s youngest ever debutant (18 years and 72 days old) when he appeared as a substitute against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] at [[Old Trafford]].{{cite web |title=Rugby League Heroes: Paul Newlove (Part 1) |url=https://www.totalrl.com/rugby-league-heroes-paul-newlove-part-1/ |website=Total Rugby League |access-date=31 July 2025 |date=22 August 2023}} Newlove appeared for [[Yorkshire rugby league team|Yorkshire]] while at [[Featherstone Rovers]]; during the [[1989–90 Rugby Football League season|1989–90 season]] against [[Lancashire rugby league team|Lancashire]].{{citation needed|date=January 2023}} Newlove represented Great Britain in 1991 and in 1992 against Papua New Guinea (sub); he was selected to go on the [[1992 Great Britain Lions tour of Australia and New Zealand]] playing three matches against Australia and one against New Zealand; in 1993 against France. Newlove also won [[Cap (sport)|cap]]s for England while at [[Featherstone Rovers]] in 1992 against Wales. He played for Great Britain while at Bradford Northern in 1993 against New Zealand (3 matches), in 1994 against France, Australia, and Australia (sub), while at St. Helens in 1997 against [[Super League (Australia)#International matches|Australia (SL)]] (3 matches), and in 1998 against New Zealand. He played for England while at Bradford Northern in 1995 against Wales, Australia (2 matches), Fiji, and Wales. In 1994, Newlove briefly retired from international rugby following a dispute over his withdrawal from the [[Rugby League World Sevens]] tournament, but was recalled to the Test side after making amends.{{cite web |title=Rugby League: Newlove back in fold: Centre returns to Test duty |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-newlove-back-in-fold-centre-returns-to-test-duty-1428023.html |website=The Independent |access-date=31 July 2025 |date=9 March 1994}} Newlove was selected to play for [[England national rugby league team|England]] in the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup|1995 World Cup]] Final at centre, scoring a try in the loss against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]]. He played for England while at St. Helens in 1996 against France. In the 1997 post season, Newlove was selected to play for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] at centre in all three matches of the [[Super League Test series]] against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]]. Newlove withdrew from the [[England national rugby league team|England]] squad prior to the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]].{{cite news |title=Sport in Brief |work=The Guardian |date=7 September 2000 |location=London |page=33 |id={{ProQuest|188637550}}}} Newlove retired from international duty in 2001.{{cite web |title=Newlove quits international arena |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/1324927.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=31 July 2025 |date=11 May 2001}} ==Honoured at Featherstone Rovers== Newlove is a Featherstone Rovers Hall of Fame inductee.{{cite web|url=http://www.pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk/news/featherstone-rovers-legends-added-to-hall-of-fame-1-8214653|title=Featherstone Rovers legends added to Hall of Fame|publisher=pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk|date=5 November 2016|access-date=1 January 2017}} ==Personal life== Newlove is from a family of rugby league players. He is the middle son of [[John Newlove (rugby league)|John Newlove]], the younger brother of [[Shaun Newlove]] who played in the 1980s and 1990s for [[Featherstone Rovers]], and the older brother of [[Richard Newlove]]. ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *(archived by web.archive.org) [https://web.archive.org/web/20070813083522/http://www.rlhalloffame.org.uk/newlove.htm RL Hall of Fame] *[http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=10&num=16049 Saints Heritage Society] *[https://www.fevarchive.co.uk/players/paul-newlove/744/ Profile] at fevarchive.co.uk *[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/1325262.stm Newlove's exit is no surprise] *(archived by web.archive.org) [https://web.archive.org/web/20101004182902/http://www.bradfordbulls.co.uk/content/History/3565/The%20Millennium%20Masters%20-%20Backs The Millennium Masters – Backs] {{s-start}} {{s-ach|ach}} {{succession box |before= [[Martin Offiah]] |title= [[Rugby league transfer record]]
